food_subgroup: Braised or stewed poultry with herbal broth

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for herbal braised chicken; FOODON_03302051 (chicken dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (chicken meat, cooked) used as closest parent; no specific herbal braised chicken code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for traditional herbal chicken as a prepared dish.

serving.description: 1 portion (approximately half a chicken with broth and herbs)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of traditional herbal chicken is approximately 350 g, comprising chicken pieces on the bone with accompanying herbal broth and softened herbs such as red dates, wolfberries, and dried longan.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, stewed with Chinese herbs — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Chinese herbal chicken / braised chicken with herbs used as primary reference. Values represent the edible portion of chicken with herbal broth. Minor adaptation applied to account for the specific herb blend (wolfberries, red dates, dang gui, huang qi) typical of the 古早味 style; core macros anchored to HPB data for stewed herbal chicken. Where HPB does not report a micronutrient, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, stewed' (FDC ID 171477) was used as a secondary reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for traditional herbal chicken. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous stewed chicken with low-GI herb and vegetable additions (red dates, wolfberries, longan). Stewed chicken dishes with minimal starchy carbohydrate consistently fall in the low GI range (GI 30–45) per University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed meat-and-herb dishes. Glycemic load per 350 g serving calculated as: (35 × 2.4 × 3.5) / 100 = 2.94, rounded to 2.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 350 g serving provides approximately 1085 mg sodium, which represents 47% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced soy sauce or consume a smaller portion and avoid drinking the full volume of broth.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 35 and only 2.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g, traditional herbal chicken has a glycemic load of under 3 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals managing blood glucose when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and cholesterol from chicken skin and sodium from seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains moderate saturated fat (2.3 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (72 mg per 100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should remove the skin before eating, limit broth intake to reduce sodium load, and consider portion control to keep saturated fat within the 20 g daily limit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Traditional Herbal Chicken (古早味药材鸡, Gu Zao Wei Yao Cai Ji) is a classic Singaporean-Chinese braised chicken dish prepared by slow-simmering whole chicken or chicken pieces in a fragrant herbal broth. The broth is built from a combination of traditional Chinese medicinal herbs and dried fruits including wolfberries (goji berries), red dates (jujube), dried longan, angelica root (dang gui), astragalus root (huang qi), Solomon's seal (yu zhu), and ginger, seasoned with soy sauce and sesame oil. The term 古早味 (gu zao wei) means 'old-fashioned flavour' or 'nostalgic taste' in Hokkien-influenced Singaporean Chinese, evoking home-style cooking passed down through generations. The dish is characterised by a deeply savoury, mildly sweet, and subtly earthy broth, with tender fall-off-the-bone chicken that has absorbed the herbal aromatics during slow cooking. Nutritionally, it is a protein-rich dish (approximately 16.8 g protein per 100 g) with moderate fat content, low carbohydrate, and a low glycemic index, making it broadly suitable for balanced diets. The herbs used are associated in traditional Chinese medicine with qi-tonifying and blood-nourishing properties. Clinically, the primary concern is sodium from soy sauce seasoning; individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should moderate broth consumption. It is commonly served as a main dish at family meals, festive occasions, and in hawker centres and zi char restaurants across Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: What herbs are in 药材鸡?
Is herbal chicken good for diabetics?
What is gu zao wei yao cai ji?
Is herbal chicken high in protein?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are anchored to HPB Singapore Foodo data for stewed herbal chicken and supplemented with USDA SR Legacy data for stewed chicken; the specific 古早味 herb blend introduces minor variation not captured in any single approved source entry. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database principles for low-carbohydrate stewed chicken dishes. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source entry exactly matches this specific dish formulation.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source entries for stewed chicken. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is a confirmed trace/negligible value consistent with unprocessed poultry and plant-based herb ingredients per USDA SR Legacy. The halal status is marked false as the dish may contain soy sauce with wheat and preparation practices vary by vendor; consumers requiring halal certification should verify with the specific restaurant. The dish is classified as minimally processed as it involves slow-cooking of whole food ingredients without industrial processing. Sodium values reflect typical restaurant preparation with soy sauce seasoning; home-prepared versions may have lower sodium. GI set to 35 as an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date.
